extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) is violated because transition t5 explicitly sets sent:=true while opted_out is true.

Transitions that set sent=true: t2 (send_attempt, guard: not opted_out) and t5 (send_attempt, guard: opted_out and ticks>=10). t5 is the culprit: its guard requires opted_out to be true, yet it assigns sent:=true, directly contradicting the safety invariant.

Counterexample execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound), starting from initial state opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0:
1-10. tick x10: ticks increments 0->1->...->10 (capped at 10 per t4), sent stays false. After 10 ticks: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=10.
11. optout (t1): opted_out:=true, sent:=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
12. send_attempt: now opted_out=true and ticks>=10, so t5 is enabled and fires: sent:=true. Final state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=10.

This final reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating (opted_out implies not sent).

Note on rule interaction: on the final send_attempt, both t3 (guard opted_out -> sent:=false, safe) and t5 (guard opted_out and ticks>=10 -> sent:=true, unsafe) are simultaneously enabled. No explicit priority is specified that would forbid t5 from firing. Since a safety property must hold on ALL executions, the existence of a reachable execution in which t5 fires is sufficient to violate the property. The intended invariant assumes opt-out always suppresses sending, but t5 reintroduces sending once ticks saturate at 10, defeating the opt-out guarantee.
